Given the structural evidence (tagMenuItem helper, id infra, and the 4 prior attempts reporting the impl as absent), this iteration appears to have completed the pattern, but the truncated region should be spot-checked to confirm (d) focus move on open and (e) the arrow-key/Escape keydown handler with btn.focus() on Escape are present and wired to the menu element.

issues.description: Verify (cannot confirm from truncated view) that openMenu() moves focus to the first [role=menuitem] after revealing the menu, and that a keydown listener on the menu element implements ArrowDown/ArrowUp focus cycling with wrap-around (and preventDefault on arrows) plus Escape that calls closeMenu() and returns focus to btn.focus(). The static ARIA attributes (aria-controls, role=menu, role=menuitem) are confirmed present and correct.

output.feedback: The visible portion of src/app/dom-builders.ts correctly implements the static ARIA improvements: a deterministic stable menu id ('row-menu-' + counter) with a resetRowMenuIds() export for render determinism, btn aria-controls pointing at menu.id, role='menu' on the .row-menu container, and role='menuitem' on the Download <a> (via setAttribute) and on each Delete/Move/Copy button via a new tagMenuItem() wrapper. A docstring documents the id-reset rationale. The code is truncated at the openMenu() body (menu.style.left = Math.m...), so the focus-management call (first menuitem .focus() after reveal) and the menu keydown handler implementing ArrowUp/Down wrap-around navigation, arrow preventDefault, and Escape-to-trigger-focus could not be visually confirmed in this review. output.summary: Re-assessed the fixes for the seven prior UI/UX findings. Confirmed resolved: viewport clamping for the dropdown (was high), the ARIA menu pattern with role=menu/menuitem, aria-controls, focus-into-menu and Arrow/Escape nav (was high), the accessible Actions header via aria-label (was high), scroll/resize dismissal (was medium), per-row unique aria-label (was medium), enlarged 24x24 target (was medium), and the keyboard :focus-visible + opacity bump for the trigger (was critical/low). However the original critical 'touch unreachable' finding is only PARTIALLY fixed: the base .row-menu-btn rule still carries pointer-events:none, so although the ⋮ button was made faintly visible (opacity 0.35) for touch, it remains untappable because none of the pointer-events:auto reveal conditions can fire before the button is first interacted with — which is impossible on a no-hover device. Two minor remaining/new gaps: menu items lack explicit focus-visible styling, and Tab does not close the open menu.

findings.title: Visible ⋮ trigger remains non-tappable on touch (pointer-events:none kept on base rule)
findings.description: The fix raised the base opacity from 0 to 0.35 specifically so touch devices 'can still perceive and tap the trigger' (per the new CSS comment), but the base `.row-menu-btn` rule still sets `pointer-events: none`. pointer-events:none blocks ALL pointer input, including touch, so on a phone/tablet the now-visible ⋮ button cannot be tapped at all. The reveal conditions that switch to `pointer-events:auto` (tr:hover, tr:focus-within, .row-menu-btn:focus-visible, [aria-expanded=true]) cannot fire without first interacting with the button — which the blocked input makes impossible. Net effect: touch users see an affordance that does nothing when tapped; the only working path is an undiscoverable long-press (contextmenu) on the row. The original critical finding's keyboard half is fixed, but the touch-operability half is not — the fix is internally inconsistent with its own stated intent.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css, make the ⋮ trigger directly operable on touch/coarse-pointer devices while preserving the desktop hover-to-reveal aesthetic. Add a media query such as `@media (hover: none), (pointer: coarse) { .row-menu-btn { opacity: 1; pointer-events: auto; } }` so that on devices without a hover-capable pointer the button is fully visible and tappable at rest (desktop keeps the existing reveal-on-hover behavior). Then verify, using browser touch/coarse-pointer emulation, that the ⋮ button both renders and responds to a single tap to open the dropdown. Optionally update src/app.css.test.ts to assert that a coarse-pointer reveal rule exists.

findings.title: Dropdown menu items rely on browser-default focus styling
findings.description: The fix added explicit `:focus-visible` styling to the trigger (.row-menu-btn:focus-visible outline 2px solid #6aa6ff), but no equivalent rule exists for the menu items (`.row-menu .btn`). Focus is moved into the menu on open and navigated with Arrow Up/Down, so the currently-focused Download/Delete/Move/Copy item must show a clearly visible focus indicator. The app's convention elsewhere is an explicit #6aa6ff ring; the menu items currently fall back to the browser default outline, which is inconsistent with the rest of the app and may be absent if a global reset sets `outline: none`. This is the remaining, unaddressed half of the prior low finding (which covered 'trigger or menu items').
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css, add an explicit focus-visible style for the dropdown menu items matching the app's #6aa6ff focus convention, e.g. `.row-menu .btn:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid #6aa6ff; outline-offset: 2px; }` (or a background treatment consistent with the existing `.row-menu .btn:hover` rule), so the focused item in the open dropdown is clearly indicated for keyboard users.

findings.title: Tab key does not dismiss the open dropdown menu
findings.description: The menu's keydown handler (onMenuKey) handles ArrowUp, ArrowDown, and Escape, but not Tab. Per the WAI-ARIA menu-button pattern, Tab closes the menu (Tab is not used to navigate within a menu). Currently, pressing Tab while focused in the menu moves focus to the next page element but leaves the dropdown open and orphaned (hidden=false, aria-expanded=true), disconnected from its trigger. A keyboard user can recover with Escape, but the lingering open menu is confusing and deviates from the expected menu interaction model.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/dom-builders.ts, in the menu's keydown handler (onMenuKey), also close the menu on Tab — e.g. `if (event.key === 'Tab') { closeMenu(); }` WITHOUT calling preventDefault (so focus naturally advances) — so the dropdown dismisses when the user tabs out of it, matching the ARIA Authoring Practices Guide menu pattern. Optionally also add Home/End handlers to jump to the first/last menuitem.

findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/dom-builders.ts, in the menu's keydown handler (onMenuKey), also close the menu on Tab — e.g. `if (event.key === 'Tab') { closeMenu(); }` WITHOUT calling preventDefault (so focus naturally advances) — so the dropdown dismisses when the user tabs out of it, matching the ARIA Authoring Practices Guide menu pattern. Optionally also add Home/End handlers to jump to the first/last menuitem.

output.summary: Re-assessed the fixes for the seven prior UI/UX findings. Confirmed resolved: viewport clamping for the dropdown (was high), the ARIA menu pattern with role=menu/menuitem, aria-controls, focus-into-menu and Arrow/Escape nav (was high), the accessible Actions header via aria-label (was high), scroll/resize dismissal (was medium), per-row unique aria-label (was medium), enlarged 24x24 target (was medium), and the keyboard :focus-visible + opacity bump for the trigger (was critical/low). However the original critical 'touch unreachable' finding is only PARTIALLY fixed: the base .row-menu-btn rule still carries pointer-events:none, so although the ⋮ button was made faintly visible (opacity 0.35) for touch, it remains untappable because none of the pointer-events:auto reveal conditions can fire before the button is first interacted with — which is impossible on a no-hover device. Two minor remaining/new gaps: menu items lack explicit focus-visible styling, and Tab does not close the open menu.

findings.title: Visible ⋮ trigger remains non-tappable on touch (pointer-events:none kept on base rule)
findings.description: The fix raised the base opacity from 0 to 0.35 specifically so touch devices 'can still perceive and tap the trigger' (per the new CSS comment), but the base `.row-menu-btn` rule still sets `pointer-events: none`. pointer-events:none blocks ALL pointer input, including touch, so on a phone/tablet the now-visible ⋮ button cannot be tapped at all. The reveal conditions that switch to `pointer-events:auto` (tr:hover, tr:focus-within, .row-menu-btn:focus-visible, [aria-expanded=true]) cannot fire without first interacting with the button — which the blocked input makes impossible. Net effect: touch users see an affordance that does nothing when tapped; the only working path is an undiscoverable long-press (contextmenu) on the row. The original critical finding's keyboard half is fixed, but the touch-operability half is not — the fix is internally inconsistent with its own stated intent.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css, make the ⋮ trigger directly operable on touch/coarse-pointer devices while preserving the desktop hover-to-reveal aesthetic. Add a media query such as `@media (hover: none), (pointer: coarse) { .row-menu-btn { opacity: 1; pointer-events: auto; } }` so that on devices without a hover-capable pointer the button is fully visible and tappable at rest (desktop keeps the existing reveal-on-hover behavior). Then verify, using browser touch/coarse-pointer emulation, that the ⋮ button both renders and responds to a single tap to open the dropdown. Optionally update src/app.css.test.ts to assert that a coarse-pointer reveal rule exists.

findings.title: Dropdown menu items rely on browser-default focus styling
findings.description: The fix added explicit `:focus-visible` styling to the trigger (.row-menu-btn:focus-visible outline 2px solid #6aa6ff), but no equivalent rule exists for the menu items (`.row-menu .btn`). Focus is moved into the menu on open and navigated with Arrow Up/Down, so the currently-focused Download/Delete/Move/Copy item must show a clearly visible focus indicator. The app's convention elsewhere is an explicit #6aa6ff ring; the menu items currently fall back to the browser default outline, which is inconsistent with the rest of the app and may be absent if a global reset sets `outline: none`. This is the remaining, unaddressed half of the prior low finding (which covered 'trigger or menu items').
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app.css, add an explicit focus-visible style for the dropdown menu items matching the app's #6aa6ff focus convention, e.g. `.row-menu .btn:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid #6aa6ff; outline-offset: 2px; }` (or a background treatment consistent with the existing `.row-menu .btn:hover` rule), so the focused item in the open dropdown is clearly indicated for keyboard users.

findings.title: Tab key does not dismiss the open dropdown menu
findings.description: The menu's keydown handler (onMenuKey) handles ArrowUp, ArrowDown, and Escape, but not Tab. Per the WAI-ARIA menu-button pattern, Tab closes the menu (Tab is not used to navigate within a menu). Currently, pressing Tab while focused in the menu moves focus to the next page element but leaves the dropdown open and orphaned (hidden=false, aria-expanded=true), disconnected from its trigger. A keyboard user can recover with Escape, but the lingering open menu is confusing and deviates from the expected menu interaction model.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/dom-builders.ts, in the menu's keydown handler (onMenuKey), also close the menu on Tab — e.g. `if (event.key === 'Tab') { closeMenu(); }` WITHOUT calling preventDefault (so focus naturally advances) — so the dropdown dismisses when the user tabs out of it, matching the ARIA Authoring Practices Guide menu pattern. Optionally also add Home/End handlers to jump to the first/last menuitem.

All 4 failing test groups are correctly implemented in production code, and the entire test suite (454 tests) is green with no regressions.

1. ARIA menu pattern & keyboard navigation (src/app/dom-builders.ts `makeRowMenu`): `role="menu"` set on `.row-menu`; a module-scoped `rowMenuIdCounter` (reset per-render via the exported `resetRowMenuIds()`, called from render-browse.ts) yields stable unique ids; `aria-controls` pairs the trigger to its menu; every action element (Download `<a>` + Delete/Move/Copy buttons) is tagged `role="menuitem"` via the `tagMenuItem` helper; `openMenu` focuses the first `[role=menuitem]`; and `onMenuKey` handles ArrowDown/ArrowUp (wrap-around, with `event.preventDefault()`) and Escape (close + `btn.focus()`). The pre-existing document-level Escape listener (`onDocKey`) and the menu-local one coexist correctly because `closeMenu()` is idempotent.

2. Per-row trigger accessible name: `aria-label` is now `'Actions for ' + entry.name`.

3. Viewport clamping: `openMenu` unhides the menu first, measures via `getBoundingClientRect()`, then clamps to `[4, innerWidth - width - 4] × [4, innerHeight - height - 4]` — exactly matching the test's expected formula.

4. CSS keyboard focus & touch (src/app.css): `.row-menu-btn:focus-visible` participates in the grouped reveal rule AND has a standalone focus-ring rule (`outline` + `outline-offset`); a brace-balanced `@media (hover: none)` block sets `opacity: 1; pointer-events: auto;` so touch users can reach the trigger.

Code quality is clean: no dead code (verified `resetRowMenuIds` is imported/used in render-browse.ts and `onDocKey` is registered/removed), no debug leftovers, no commented-out code, names match behavior, functions stay single-responsibility, and docstrings document the new public/changed behavior. The implementation integrates with existing conventions (e.g., the `tagMenuItem` helper mirrors the existing `makeActionButton` pattern; string interpolation continues to avoid innerHTML).

output.summary: VERIFIED FIXES: The critical finding (red test suite from ~27 tests asserting unimplemented behavior) is FULLY RESOLVED — `npx vitest run` reports 454/454 passing. All 4 previously-unimplemented feature areas now have complete production code: (1) ARIA menu pattern (role=menu, role=menuitem, aria-controls, focus-to-first-item on open, ArrowUp/Down wrap-around nav, Escape→focus-trigger) in dom-builders.ts makeRowMenu; (2) per-row aria-label="Actions for <name>"; (3) viewport clamping in openMenu via Math.max/min against innerWidth/innerHeight; (4) CSS :focus-visible rules + @media (hover: none) touch operability in app.css. The prior high finding (CSS test quote-mismatch) remains fixed. No regressions detected — the implementation is clean with proper listener teardown, idempotent closeMenu, deterministic id generation via resetRowMenuIds, and good doc comments. REMAINING: the two prior low findings (stale migration-referencing comments, aria-haspopup=legacy 'true') are STILL unaddressed despite being reported three times. One new low doc nit (README step 8.5 non-sequential numbering). All remaining findings are low severity.

findings.title: Comments still reference former/replaced inline-button behavior — will read as stale migration notes
findings.description: Reported in the initial review, pass 1, and pass 2; still not addressed. Multiple comments describe the migration from old inline buttons to the dropdown rather than the current code's intent: (1) dom-builders.ts:144 doc-comment — 'The action-item handler bodies are identical to the former inline buttons ... so all behavior is preserved. The items keep class="btn" ... so existing query/characterization assertions hold.'; (2) dom-builders.ts:170 inline — 'Menu items — same handlers/attributes as the former inline controls.'; (3) render-browse.test.ts:680 — 'The inline Download/Delete/Move/Copy controls are replaced by a single ...'; (4) app.css:367 — 'so query-* / dataset tests that assert className work unchanged.' Once merged, 'former'/'replaced'/'unchanged' have no referent for a new reader and read as stale migration notes rather than documentation of current intent. Each should be restated to describe WHY the items carry class="btn" and why the handlers look the way they do, without referencing the prior implementation.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/dom-builders.ts, restate the doc-comment paragraph (around line 144) that says 'The action-item handler bodies are identical to the former inline buttons ... so all behavior is preserved. The items keep class="btn" ... so existing query/characterization assertions hold.' to describe current intent only — e.g. 'Each action item is a .btn element carrying role=menuitem; the .row-menu .btn CSS rule restyles it as a menu item. Handlers use window.confirm/prompt, getApi().delete/move/copy, normalizeRelativePath, and rerender() to perform the action then refresh the table.' Also restate the inline comment at line 170 ('Menu items — same handlers/attributes as the former inline controls.') to 'Menu items — Download (files only), then Delete, Move, Copy.'. In src/app/render-browse.test.ts around line 680, restate 'The inline Download/Delete/Move/Copy controls are replaced by a single ...' to describe the current structure without 'replaced'. In src/app.css around line 367, restate 'so query-* / dataset tests that assert className work unchanged' to 'so the .btn class is available for query selectors while the .row-menu .btn rule restyles them as menu items.'. Do not change any code logic — only comments.

findings.title: aria-haspopup still set to legacy 'true' instead of canonical 'menu' token
findings.description: Reported in the initial review, pass 1, and pass 2; still not addressed. The row-menu trigger button sets btn.setAttribute('aria-haspopup', 'true') at dom-builders.ts:159. Per ARIA 1.2, aria-haspopup="true" is a legacy alias equivalent to 'menu'; for a dropdown that opens a list of action choices (which now has role=menu), the canonical, self-documenting value is aria-haspopup="menu". Assistive technologies accept both, but 'menu' is the modern token and is consistent with the new role=menu on the popup. Tests at render-browse.test.ts:738 and :881 assert toBe('true') and must be updated in lockstep.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/dom-builders.ts line 159, change btn.setAttribute('aria-haspopup', 'true') to btn.setAttribute('aria-haspopup', 'menu'). In src/app/render-browse.test.ts, update the two assertions that check aria-haspopup: around line 738 change expect(btn.getAttribute('aria-haspopup')).toBe('true') to .toBe('menu'), and around line 881 do the same. Also update the test description strings at lines 725 and 873 that say aria-haspopup="true" to aria-haspopup="menu". Run npx vitest run to confirm all tests still pass.

findings.title: README verification checklist uses non-sequential step number '8.5'

findings.title: aria-haspopup still set to legacy 'true' instead of canonical 'menu' token
findings.description: Reported in the initial review, pass 1, and pass 2; still not addressed. The row-menu trigger button sets btn.setAttribute('aria-haspopup', 'true') at dom-builders.ts:159. Per ARIA 1.2, aria-haspopup="true" is a legacy alias equivalent to 'menu'; for a dropdown that opens a list of action choices (which now has role=menu), the canonical, self-documenting value is aria-haspopup="menu". Tests at render-browse.test.ts:738 and :881 assert toBe('true') and must be updated in lockstep.

findings.description: Reported in the initial review, pass 1, and pass 2; still not addressed. Multiple comments describe the migration from old inline buttons to the dropdown rather than the current code's intent: (1) dom-builders.ts:144 doc-comment — 'The action-item handler bodies are identical to the former inline buttons ... so all behavior is preserved. The items keep class="btn" ... so existing query/characterization assertions hold.'; (2) dom-builders.ts:170 inline — 'Menu items — same handlers/attributes as the former inline controls.'; (3) render-browse.test.ts:680 — 'The inline Download/Delete/Move/Copy controls are replaced by a single ...'; (4) app.css:367 — 'so query-* / dataset tests that assert className work unchanged.' Once merged, 'former'/'replaced'/'unchanged' have no referent for a new reader and read as stale migration notes rather than documentation of current intent. Each should be restated to describe WHY the items carry class="btn" and why the handlers look the way they do, without referencing the prior implementation.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/dom-builders.ts, restate the doc-comment paragraph (around line 144) that says 'The action-item handler bodies are identical to the former inline buttons ... so all behavior is preserved. The items keep class="btn" ... so existing query/characterization assertions hold.' to describe current intent only — e.g. 'Each action item is a .btn element carrying role=menuitem; the .row-menu .btn CSS rule restyles it as a menu item. Handlers use window.confirm/prompt, getApi().delete/move/copy, normalizeRelativePath, and rerender() to perform the action then refresh the table.' Also restate the inline comment at line 170 ('Menu items — same handlers/attributes as the former inline controls.') to 'Menu items — Download (files only), then Delete, Move, Copy.'. In src/app/render-browse.test.ts around line 680, restate 'The inline Download/Delete/Move/Copy controls are replaced by a single ...' to describe the current structure without 'replaced'. In src/app.css around line 367, restate 'so query-* / dataset tests that assert className work unchanged' to 'so the .btn class is available for query selectors while the .row-menu .btn rule restyles them as menu items.'. Do not change any code logic — only comments.

findings.title: aria-haspopup still set to legacy 'true' instead of canonical 'menu' token
findings.description: Reported in the initial review, pass 1, and pass 2; still not addressed. The row-menu trigger button sets btn.setAttribute('aria-haspopup', 'true') at dom-builders.ts:159. Per ARIA 1.2, aria-haspopup="true" is a legacy alias equivalent to 'menu'; for a dropdown that opens a list of action choices (which now has role=menu), the canonical, self-documenting value is aria-haspopup="menu". Assistive technologies accept both, but 'menu' is the modern token and is consistent with the new role=menu on the popup. Tests at render-browse.test.ts:738 and :881 assert toBe('true') and must be updated in lockstep.
findings.fixPrompt: In src/app/dom-builders.ts line 159, change btn.setAttribute('aria-haspopup', 'true') to btn.setAttribute('aria-haspopup', 'menu'). In src/app/render-browse.test.ts, update the two assertions that check aria-haspopup: around line 738 change expect(btn.getAttribute('aria-haspopup')).toBe('true') to .toBe('menu'), and around line 881 do the same. Also update the test description strings at lines 725 and 873 that say aria-haspopup="true" to aria-haspopup="menu". Run npx vitest run to confirm all tests still pass.

findings.title: README verification checklist uses non-sequential step number '8.5'
findings.description: The README verification checklist inserts a new step labeled '8.5. Row actions menu' between steps 8 and 9, breaking the sequential integer numbering (1–13). Non-integer step numbers are unusual in a checklist and look like an edit artifact. The steps should be renumbered to sequential integers (the row-actions-menu step becomes 9, and Download/Delete/Move/Copy/Search shift to 10–14).
findings.fixPrompt: In README.md, renumber the verification checklist steps so there is no '8.5'. The current '8.5. Row actions menu' should become '9.', and every subsequent step number increments by one: Download becomes 10, Delete 11, Move 12, Copy 13, Search 14. Update only the leading number+period of each list item; do not change the step descriptions.

output.summary: VERIFIED FIXES: The critical finding (red test suite from ~27 tests asserting unimplemented behavior) is FULLY RESOLVED — `npx vitest run` reports 454/454 passing. All 4 previously-unimplemented feature areas now have complete production code: (1) ARIA menu pattern (role=menu, role=menuitem, aria-controls, focus-to-first-item on open, ArrowUp/Down wrap-around nav, Escape→focus-trigger) in dom-builders.ts makeRowMenu; (2) per-row aria-label="Actions for <name>"; (3) viewport clamping in openMenu via Math.max/min against innerWidth/innerHeight; (4) CSS :focus-visible rules + @media (hover: none) touch operability in app.css. The prior high finding (CSS test quote-mismatch) remains fixed. No regressions detected — the implementation is clean with proper listener teardown, idempotent closeMenu, deterministic id generation via resetRowMenuIds, and good doc comments. REMAINING: the two prior low findings (stale migration-referencing comments, aria-haspopup=legacy 'true') are STILL unaddressed despite being reported three times. One new low doc nit (README step 8.5 non-sequential numbering). All remaining findings are low severity.

output.feedback: The fix is correctly implemented and thoroughly tested. The @media (hover: none) block at lines 386-393 of src/app.css properly overrides the base .row-menu-btn's opacity:1 and pointer-events:auto for touch devices, while preserving the desktop hover-to-reveal aesthetic. All 32 CSS tests pass, including the 4 dedicated touch operability tests that verify: (1) a media query targeting coarse-pointer/touch devices exists, (2) opacity:1 at rest on touch, (3) pointer-events:auto at rest on touch, and (4) no regression of the desktop base rule. The existing test helper `coarsePointerMediaQueryBody()` correctly handles both `hover:none` and `pointer:coarse` media conditions. The only minor documentation inconsistency is the base .row-menu-btn comment saying 'touch devices... can still perceive and tap the trigger' while pointer-events:none is still set on the base rule, but the actual media query fix correctly addresses the tappable issue.

The fix fully resolves the touch-operability finding. The base `.row-menu-btn` rule still sets `opacity: 0.35; pointer-events: none` (desktop reveal-on-hover preserved), and a new `@media (hover: none), (pointer: coarse) { .row-menu-btn { opacity: 1; pointer-events: auto; } }` block now makes the ⋮ trigger fully visible AND directly tappable at rest on coarse-pointer/no-hover devices — the exact change the finding's fix instructions specified. The crux (`pointer-events: auto` inside the media query, which unblocks taps that the base `pointer-events: none` was blocking) is present and correct. Both `(hover: none)` and `(pointer: coarse)` are listed, covering touch laptops/tablets and coarse-pointer emulation. The accompanying test additions are thorough: a brace-balanced `coarsePointerMediaQueryBody()` helper plus a dedicated describe block that asserts the media query exists, lists `(pointer: coarse)` (not just `(hover: none)`), sets `opacity:1` and `pointer-events:auto` at rest on touch, and does NOT regress the desktop base rule. All 33 tests pass and Prettier formatting is clean. One minor cleanliness note: the comment on the base `.row-menu-btn` rule still claims the faint opacity is there so touch devices can 'perceive and tap the trigger', but the base rule itself sets `pointer-events: none`, so the faint visibility only provides perception — the actual tappability is delivered solely by the separate media query ~55 lines below. The 'tap' half of that comment is therefore slightly misleading relative to the base rule, though it does not affect correctness.

issues.description: The base `.row-menu-btn` comment states the faint default opacity exists 'so touch devices (which have no :hover state) can still perceive and tap the trigger'. However, the base rule keeps `pointer-events: none`, so the faint opacity only makes the button perceivable — not tappable. The tappability is delivered exclusively by the separate `@media (hover: none), (pointer: coarse)` block further down, which this base-rule comment does not reference. The 'tap' claim therefore slightly mis-describes what the base opacity achieves. Consider rewording (e.g. 'perceive' only) or noting that tapping is handled by the coarse-pointer media query below.

Both `(hover: none)` and `(pointer: coarse)` are listed, covering touch laptops/tablets and coarse-pointer emulation. The accompanying test additions are thorough: a brace-balanced `coarsePointerMediaQueryBody()` helper plus a dedicated describe block that asserts the media query exists, lists `(pointer: coarse)` (not just `(hover: none)`), sets `opacity:1` and `pointer-events:auto` at rest on touch, and does NOT regress the desktop base rule. All 33 tests pass and Prettier formatting is clean. One minor cleanliness note: the comment on the base `.row-menu-btn` rule still claims the faint opacity is there so touch devices can 'perceive and tap the trigger', but the base rule itself sets `pointer-events: none`, so the faint visibility only provides perception — the actual tappability is delivered solely by the separate media query ~55 lines below. The 'tap' half of that comment is therefore slightly misleading relative to the base rule, though it does not affect correctness.

issues.description: The base `.row-menu-btn` comment states the faint default opacity exists 'so touch devices (which have no :hover state) can still perceive and tap the trigger'. However, the base rule keeps `pointer-events: none`, so the faint opacity only makes the button perceivable — not tappable. The tappability is delivered exclusively by the separate `@media (hover: none), (pointer: coarse)` block further down, which this base-rule comment does not reference. The 'tap' claim therefore slightly mis-describes what the base opacity achieves. Consider rewording (e.g. 'perceive' only) or noting that tapping is handled by the coarse-pointer media query below.

output.summary: Re-assessed the fixer's pass-2 changes against the prior UI/UX findings. CONFIRMED RESOLVED: the [high] touch-operability defect — the new `@media (hover: none), (pointer: coarse)` block in app.css lifts `.row-menu-btn` to `opacity:1` AND `pointer-events:auto` at rest on touch, so the previously-visible-but-dead trigger is now tappable; the `(pointer: coarse)` condition correctly covers touch laptops/emulation. Also re-verified still-fixed: viewport clamping, ARIA menu pattern (role=menu/menuitem, aria-controls, focus-into-menu, Arrow/Escape nav), accessible Actions header, scroll/resize dismissal, per-row unique aria-label, 24x24 target, and the trigger :focus-visible ring. All 125 tests in the two touched test files pass. The fixer did NOT address the two low-severity items carried over from pass 1: menu-item focus styling and Tab dismissal. No new regressions were introduced — the media query, id-counter reset, and clamping logic are clean and additive.

findings.title: Dropdown menu items still have no explicit :focus-visible styling
findings.description: The fixer added explicit `:focus-visible` styling to the trigger (`.row-menu-btn:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid #6aa6ff; outline-offset: 2px; }`) but did NOT add an equivalent rule for the menu items (`.row-menu .btn`). On open, focus is moved into the menu (`first?.focus()` in openMenu) and Arrow Up/Down move focus between the Download/Delete/Move/Copy items, so the currently-focused item MUST show a clearly visible focus indicator. Currently the only feedback is `.row-menu .btn:hover { background: #3a3d41; }`, which fires on mouse hover but NOT on keyboard focus. The app's convention elsewhere (e.g. `.toolbar input:focus` border-color #6aa6ff, `.toolbar label.btn:focus-within` outline 2px solid #6aa6ff, `.row-menu-btn:focus-visible`) is an explicit #6aa6ff affordance; the menu items fall back to whatever the browser default outline is, which is inconsistent with the rest of the app and may be faint or absent depending on the UA. This is the unresolved half of the prior 'trigger or menu items' low finding.

findings.title: Tab key still does not dismiss the open dropdown menu
findings.description: The `onMenuKey` keydown handler on the menu handles `ArrowDown`, `ArrowUp`, and `Escape`, but not `Tab`/`Shift+Tab`. Per the WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices Guide menu-button pattern, Tab is not used to navigate WITHIN a menu — instead Tab closes the menu and moves focus to the next element in the page tab sequence. Currently, pressing Tab while focused in the open menu moves focus to the next focusable page element but leaves the dropdown open and orphaned (`menu.hidden === false`, trigger `aria-expanded="true"`), disconnected from its trigger. A keyboard user can recover with Escape, but the lingering open menu is confusing and deviates from the expected menu interaction model. This was reported in pass 1 and was not addressed.
